metadata.description: Training examples for GEPA skill optimization based on Claude Agent Skills best practices

examples.title: PDF Text Extraction - Remove unnecessary explanation
examples.principle: Claude is already very smart. Only add context Claude doesn't already have.

bad.content:
## Extract PDF text

PDF (Portable Document Format) files are a common file format that contains
text, images, and other content. To extract text from a PDF, you'll need to
use a library. There are many libraries available for PDF processing, but
pdfplumber is recommended because it's easy to use and handles most cases well.
First, you'll need to install it using pip. Then you can use the code below...
issues: Explains what PDFs are (Claude knows this)
Explains that libraries exist (Claude knows this)
Uses filler phrases like 'you'll need to'

good.content:
## Extract PDF text

Use pdfplumber for text extraction:

```python
import pdfplumber

with pdfplumber.open("file.pdf") as pdf:
    text = pdf.pages[0].extract_text()
```
improvements: Assumes Claude knows what PDFs are
Assumes Claude knows how libraries work
Gets straight to the actionable code

examples.title: Description Point of View - Use third person
examples.principle: Always write in third person. The description is injected into the system prompt.

bad.content: description: I can help you process Excel files

good.content: description: Processes Excel files and generates reports
improvements: Describes what the skill does objectively

examples.title: Description Point of View - Avoid second person
examples.principle: Always write in third person. The description is injected into the system prompt.

bad.content: description: You can use this to process Excel files

good.content: description: Processes Excel files and generates reports
improvements: Describes what the skill does objectively

examples.title: Description Specificity - Be specific with triggers
examples.principle: Include both what the Skill does and specific triggers/contexts for when to use it.

bad.content: description: Helps with documents
issues: Claude can't determine when to use this skill

good.content: description: Extract text and tables from PDF files, fill forms, merge documents. Use when working with PDF files or when the user mentions PDFs, forms, or document extraction.

examples.title: Description Specificity - Avoid generic descriptions
examples.principle: Be specific and include key terms.

bad.content: description: Processes data
issues: No indication of what kind of data

good.content: description: Analyze Excel spreadsheets, create pivot tables, generate charts. Use when analyzing Excel files, spreadsheets, tabular data, or .xlsx files.

examples.title: Description Specificity - Meaningful descriptions
examples.principle: Your description must provide enough detail for Claude to know when to select this Skill.

bad.content: description: Does stuff with files

good.content: description: Generate descriptive commit messages by analyzing git diffs. Use when the user asks for help writing commit messages or reviewing staged changes.

examples.title: Skill Naming - Use gerund form
examples.principle: Use gerund form (verb + -ing) as this clearly describes the activity or capability.

bad.content:
name: helper
name: utils
name: tools
name: documents
name: data
name: files
issues: Don't describe what the skill does

good.content:
name: processing-pdfs
name: analyzing-spreadsheets
name: managing-databases
name: testing-code
name: writing-documentation

examples.title: Skill Naming - Avoid reserved words
examples.principle: Cannot contain reserved words: 'anthropic', 'claude'

bad.content:
name: anthropic-helper
name: claude-tools

good.content:
name: pdf-processing
name: spreadsheet-analysis

examples.title: Reference Depth - Keep one level deep
examples.principle: Keep references one level deep from SKILL.md to ensure Claude reads complete files.

bad.content:
# SKILL.md
See [advanced.md](advanced.md)...

# advanced.md
See [details.md](details.md)...

# details.md
Here's the actual information...
issues: Claude may only partially read files

good.content:
# SKILL.md

**Basic usage**: [instructions in SKILL.md]
**Advanced features**: See [advanced.md](advanced.md)
**API reference**: See [reference.md](reference.md)
**Examples**: See [examples.md](examples.md)

examples.title: Time-Sensitive Information - Use old patterns section
examples.principle: Don't include information that will become outdated.

bad.content:
If you're doing this before August 2025, use the old API.
After August 2025, use the new API.

good.content:
## Current method

Use the v2 API endpoint: `api.example.com/v2/messages`

## Old patterns

<details>
<summary>Legacy v1 API (deprecated 2025-08)</summary>

The v1 API used: `api.example.com/v1/messages`

This endpoint is no longer supported.
</details>

examples.title: Too Many Options - Provide a default
examples.principle: Don't present multiple approaches unless necessary.

bad.content: You can use pypdf, or pdfplumber, or PyMuPDF, or pdf2image, or...

good.content:
Use pdfplumber for text extraction:
```python
import pdfplumber
```

For scanned PDFs requiring OCR, use pdf2image with pytesseract instead.

examples.title: File Paths - Use Unix-style
examples.principle: Always use forward slashes in file paths, even on Windows.

bad.content:
scripts\helper.py
reference\guide.md

good.content:
scripts/helper.py
reference/guide.md

examples.title: Error Handling - Don't punt to Claude
examples.principle: Handle error conditions rather than punting to Claude.

examples.title: Magic Numbers - Document constants
examples.principle: Configuration parameters should be justified and documented to avoid 'voodoo constants'.

bad.content:
TIMEOUT = 47  # Why 47?
RETRIES = 5  # Why 5?

good.content:
# HTTP requests typically complete within 30 seconds
# Longer timeout accounts for slow connections
REQUEST_TIMEOUT = 30

# Three retries balances reliability vs speed
# Most intermittent failures resolve by the second retry
MAX_RETRIES = 3

examples.title: Package Dependencies - Be explicit
examples.principle: Don't assume packages are available.

bad.content: Use the pdf library to process the file.

good.content:
Install required package: `pip install pypdf`

Then use it:
```python
from pypdf import PdfReader
reader = PdfReader("file.pdf")
```

examples.title: Terminology - Be consistent
examples.principle: Choose one term and use it throughout the Skill.

bad.content:
Mix of terms:
- 'API endpoint', 'URL', 'API route', 'path'
- 'field', 'box', 'element', 'control'
- 'extract', 'pull', 'get', 'retrieve'

good.content:
Consistent terms:
- Always 'API endpoint'
- Always 'field'
- Always 'extract'

examples.title: File Naming - Use descriptive names
examples.principle: Name files descriptively to indicate content.

bad.content:
docs/file1.md
docs/file2.md
docs/doc2.md

good.content:
reference/finance.md
reference/sales.md
form_validation_rules.md

examples.title: Table of Contents - Add for long files
examples.principle: For reference files longer than 100 lines, include a table of contents at the top.

bad.content:
# API Reference

## Authentication and setup
...

## Core methods
...

## Advanced features
...
issues: Claude may miss sections with partial reads

good.content:
# API Reference

## Contents
- Authentication and setup
- Core methods (create, read, update, delete)
- Advanced features (batch operations, webhooks)
- Error handling patterns
- Code examples

## Authentication and setup
...

## Core methods
...

examples.title: Workflows - Use checklists for complex tasks
examples.principle: Break complex operations into clear, sequential steps with a checklist.

bad.content: To fill the PDF form, analyze it, then map fields, validate, fill, and verify.

good.content:
## PDF form filling workflow

Copy this checklist and check off items as you complete them:

```
Task Progress:
- [ ] Step 1: Analyze the form (run analyze_form.py)
- [ ] Step 2: Create field mapping (edit fields.json)
- [ ] Step 3: Validate mapping (run validate_fields.py)
- [ ] Step 4: Fill the form (run fill_form.py)
- [ ] Step 5: Verify output (run verify_output.py)
```

**Step 1: Analyze the form**

Run: `python scripts/analyze_form.py input.pdf`

This extracts form fields and their locations, saving to `fields.json`.

examples.title: Feedback Loops - Implement validation
examples.principle: Run validator → fix errors → repeat. This pattern greatly improves output quality.

bad.content: Make your edits to the document and save it.

good.content:
## Document editing process

1. Make your edits to `word/document.xml`
2. **Validate immediately**: `python ooxml/scripts/validate.py unpacked_dir/`
3. If validation fails:
   - Review the error message carefully
   - Fix the issues in the XML
   - Run validation again
4. **Only proceed when validation passes**
5. Rebuild: `python ooxml/scripts/pack.py unpacked_dir/ output.docx`
6. Test the output document

examples.title: Templates - Strict format for critical output
examples.principle: For strict requirements, provide exact template structure.

bad.content: Generate a report with a summary, findings, and recommendations.

good.content:
## Report structure

ALWAYS use this exact template structure:

```markdown
# [Analysis Title]

## Executive summary
[One-paragraph overview of key findings]

## Key findings
- Finding 1 with supporting data
- Finding 2 with supporting data
- Finding 3 with supporting data

## Recommendations
1. Specific actionable recommendation
2. Specific actionable recommendation
```

examples.title: Templates - Flexible guidance when adaptation needed
examples.principle: For flexible guidance, provide sensible defaults but allow adaptation.

bad.content: ALWAYS use this exact format for all reports no matter what.

good.content:
## Report structure

Here is a sensible default format, but use your best judgment based on the analysis:

```markdown
# [Analysis Title]

## Executive summary
[Overview]

## Key findings
[Adapt sections based on what you discover]

## Recommendations
[Tailor to the specific context]
```

Adjust sections as needed for the specific analysis type.

examples.title: Filler Phrases - Remove unnecessary words
examples.principle: Remove filler phrases that add no value.

bad.content: Make sure to install kubectl using the appropriate method for your operating system. Ensure that you download the correct version. It is important to verify the installation by running the version command. Don't forget to check that you have the necessary permissions. Remember to configure your kubeconfig file properly. You should always validate your YAML syntax before applying configurations. You need to ensure that you have access to a Kubernetes cluster.
issues: Multiple filler phrases: 'Make sure to', 'Ensure that', 'It is important to'
'Don't forget to', 'Remember to', 'You should', 'You need to'

good.content:
## Prerequisites

- Install `kubectl` for your OS and verify with `kubectl version`
- Access to a Kubernetes cluster with configured `kubeconfig`
- Validate YAML syntax before applying configurations

examples.title: Acronym Expansion - Don't explain common terms
examples.principle: Claude already knows common technical concepts.

bad.content:
YAML (Yet Another Markup Language, or sometimes referred to as YAML Ain't Markup Language) is a human-readable data serialization format that is commonly used for configuration files.

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is another data interchange format that you might encounter.

API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of protocols and tools for building software applications.
issues: Claude already knows YAML, JSON, API

good.content: Kubernetes accepts both YAML and JSON for configuration files.

examples.title: MCP Tool References - Use fully qualified names
examples.principle: Always use fully qualified tool names to avoid 'tool not found' errors.

bad.content:
Use the bigquery_schema tool to retrieve table schemas.
Use the create_issue tool to create issues.
issues: Ambiguous when multiple MCP servers available

good.content:
Use the BigQuery:bigquery_schema tool to retrieve table schemas.
Use the GitHub:create_issue tool to create issues.

examples.title: Degrees of Freedom - Match specificity to task fragility
examples.principle: Match the level of specificity to the task's fragility and variability.

high_freedom.content:
## Code review process

1. Analyze the code structure and organization
2. Check for potential bugs or edge cases
3. Suggest improvements for readability and maintainability
4. Verify adherence to project conventions

medium_freedom.content:
## Generate report

Use this template and customize as needed:

```python
def generate_report(data, format="markdown", include_charts=True):
    # Process data
    # Generate output in specified format
    # Optionally include visualizations
```

use_when: A specific sequence must be followed
low_freedom.content:
## Database migration

Run exactly this script:

```bash
python scripts/migrate.py --verify --backup
```

Do not modify the command or add additional flags.

examples.title: Conditional Workflow - Guide through decision points
examples.principle: Guide Claude through decision points with clear branching.

bad.content: Create or edit the document as needed.

good.content:
## Document modification workflow

1. Determine the modification type:

   **Creating new content?** → Follow "Creation workflow" below
   **Editing existing content?** → Follow "Editing workflow" below

2. Creation workflow:
   - Use docx-js library
   - Build document from scratch
   - Export to .docx format

3. Editing workflow:
   - Unpack existing document
   - Modify XML directly
   - Validate after each change
   - Repack when complete

examples.title: Examples Pattern - Provide input/output pairs
examples.principle: Provide input/output pairs just like in regular prompting.

bad.content: Generate good commit messages following conventional commits format.

good.content:
## Commit message format

Generate commit messages following these examples:

**Example 1:**
Input: Added user authentication with JWT tokens
Output:
```
feat(auth): implement JWT-based authentication

Add login endpoint and token validation middleware
```

**Example 2:**
Input: Fixed bug where dates displayed incorrectly in reports
Output:
```
fix(reports): correct date formatting in timezone conversion

Use UTC timestamps consistently across report generation
```

**Example 3:**
Input: Updated dependencies and refactored error handling
Output:
```
chore: update dependencies and refactor error handling

- Upgrade lodash to 4.17.21
- Standardize error response format across endpoints
```

Follow this style: type(scope): brief description, then detailed explanation.

examples.title: Progressive Disclosure - High-level guide with references
examples.principle: SKILL.md serves as an overview that points Claude to detailed materials as needed.

bad.content:
# PDF Processing

[500+ lines of detailed documentation all in one file covering extraction, forms, merging, API reference, examples, troubleshooting...]

good.content:
---
name: pdf-processing
description: Extracts text and tables from PDF files, fills forms, and merges documents. Use when working with PDF files or when the user mentions PDFs, forms, or document extraction.
---

# PDF Processing

## Quick start

Extract text with pdfplumber:
```python
import pdfplumber
with pdfplumber.open("file.pdf") as pdf:
    text = pdf.pages[0].extract_text()
```

## Advanced features

**Form filling**: See [FORMS.md](FORMS.md) for complete guide
**API reference**: See [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md) for all methods
**Examples**: See [EXAMPLES.md](EXAMPLES.md) for common patterns

examples.title: Domain Organization - Organize by domain
examples.principle: Organize content by domain to avoid loading irrelevant context.

bad.content:
# BigQuery Data Analysis

[All table schemas for finance, sales, product, and marketing in one giant file]

good.content:
# BigQuery Data Analysis

## Available datasets

**Finance**: Revenue, ARR, billing → See [reference/finance.md](reference/finance.md)
**Sales**: Opportunities, pipeline, accounts → See [reference/sales.md](reference/sales.md)
**Product**: API usage, features, adoption → See [reference/product.md](reference/product.md)
**Marketing**: Campaigns, attribution, email → See [reference/marketing.md](reference/marketing.md)

## Quick search

Find specific metrics using grep:

```bash
grep -i "revenue" reference/finance.md
grep -i "pipeline" reference/sales.md
grep -i "api usage" reference/product.md
```
